content:
# Formation Damage

**Summary**: The reduction in permeability or injectivity of a formation.

---

## How It Appears in the Thesis Collection

- [[2012-al-harbi|Al-Harbi, 2012]] studied the impact of precipitates on well productivity.

- [[2016-jad|Jad, 2016]] studied formation damage caused by drilling, acidizing, and EOR chemicals.

- [[2017-mohamed|MOHAMED, 2017]] investigated formation damage caused by solid particles and fluid filtrate in HPHT wells.

- [[2021-gasimli|Gasimli, 2021]] evaluated formation-damage caused by iron sulfide scale precipitation.

## Scientific Context

- [[2012-al-harbi|Al-Harbi, 2012]] A critical concern in acidizing operations.

- [[2016-jad|Jad, 2016]] studied formation damage caused by drilling, acidizing, and EOR chemicals.

- [[2017-mohamed|MOHAMED, 2017]] investigated formation damage caused by solid particles and fluid filtrate in HPHT wells.

- [[2021-gasimli|Gasimli, 2021]] evaluated formation-damage caused by iron sulfide scale precipitation.

## Measurement and Characterization Methods

- [[2012-al-harbi|Al-Harbi, 2012]] Core flood analysis

## Key Findings Across Sources

- AlF3 precipitation is a major cause of damage in acid treatments. ([[2012-al-harbi|Al-Harbi, 2012, p. 45]])


- Damage was identified as being caused by fines migration and precipitation of reaction products in sandstone, and VES adsorption on carbonate. ([[2016-jad|Jad, 2016, p. 15]])

- Solids from conventional drill-in fluids were identified as a primary cause of permeability reduction. ([[2017-mohamed|MOHAMED, 2017, p. 19]])

- Iron sulfide minerals with sizes up to 296 μm can block pore throats as small as 19 μm. ([[2021-gasimli|Gasimli, 2021, p. 54]])
